title: Remarks at the World Bank Group Annual Meetings 2023

paragraphs.text: Good morning, everyone, and thank you for joining us here in Marrakech. I want to begin by expressing our solidarity with the people of Morocco following the devastating earthquake that struck last month. The resilience and determination of the Moroccan people in the face of this tragedy is deeply inspiring, and the World Bank Group stands ready to support reconstruction and recovery efforts.

I have now been President of the World Bank Group for just over five months, and in that time I have traveled to more than twenty countries across every region. Let me tell you what I have seen: I have seen the devastating impact of climate change on communities that did virtually nothing to cause it. I have seen the crushing weight of debt on nations trying to invest in their people. And I have seen extraordinary innovation and entrepreneurial energy waiting to be unleashed if only the right conditions are created.

The mission of the World Bank Group has never been more urgent or more relevant. We exist to end extreme poverty and boost shared prosperity on a livable planet. That last phrase — a livable planet — is new, and it is deliberate. We cannot achieve our development goals on a planet ravaged by climate change, biodiversity loss, and environmental degradation. Climate and development are not competing priorities; they are inextricably linked.

The numbers tell a sobering story. An estimated seven hundred million people still live in extreme poverty. Progress on reducing poverty has stalled in many countries, and in some it has reversed. The pandemic set back development gains by years, and the compounding crises of inflation, food insecurity, and conflict have made recovery even harder. We are not on track to meet the Sustainable Development Goals by 2030, and we need to be honest about that.

At the same time, the climate crisis is accelerating. The science tells us that to limit warming to one point five degrees, we need to cut global emissions by forty-three percent by 2030. We are nowhere close. Climate impacts are already costing developing countries billions of dollars annually in damage to infrastructure, agriculture, and health. The World Bank's own analysis suggests that by 2030, climate change could push an additional one hundred thirty million people into poverty.

This is why I have been pushing for a fundamental evolution of the World Bank Group since my first day in office. We need to be bigger, better, and faster. Bigger in the volume of financing we can mobilize. Better in the impact of every dollar we deploy. And faster in how we get resources to the countries and communities that need them.

On the bigger front, we have taken concrete steps to stretch our balance sheet. By optimizing our capital adequacy framework, we can generate up to an additional fifty billion dollars in lending capacity over the next decade without asking shareholders for new capital. We have also introduced portfolio guarantee instruments and hybrid capital mechanisms that allow us to do more with the resources we have. But let me be clear: balance sheet optimization alone is not sufficient to close the financing gap.

The private sector holds the key to the scale of investment that is needed. Developing countries require an estimated two point four trillion dollars annually in additional investment to meet their climate and development goals. Public finance — from bilateral donors, multilateral institutions, and domestic budgets combined — can only provide a fraction of that amount. We must find ways to crowd in private capital at unprecedented scale.

This is why I have made private capital mobilization a central pillar of our evolution agenda. The International Finance Corporation and the Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency — our private sector arms — must be at the forefront of this effort. We are developing new platforms that aggregate projects, standardize documentation, and create investment-grade opportunities that institutional investors can participate in. Pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and insurance companies manage trillions of dollars in assets — even a small reallocation toward emerging market infrastructure and climate investments would be transformative.

But private capital will not flow to places where the risk-return profile is unattractive. This is where the World Bank Group's role as a de-risking partner is critical. Through blended finance structures, first-loss tranches, political risk insurance, and policy reform support, we can bridge the gap between the risk appetite of private investors and the realities of investing in developing economies. We need to move from billions to trillions, and that requires a fundamentally different approach to how we work with the private sector.

On the better front, we are overhauling how we measure results. It is not enough to count the dollars we lend; we must measure the outcomes we achieve. Are children learning? Are health systems delivering? Are emissions declining? Are small businesses growing? I have asked every part of the World Bank Group to adopt rigorous results frameworks that track impact, not just inputs and outputs.

We are also reforming our approach to country engagement. Every country is different, and our support must be tailored to specific contexts and priorities. I want the World Bank to be the partner of first resort for developing countries — the institution that brings not just financing but knowledge, data, convening power, and implementation support. We have decades of experience in what works and what does not, and we must deploy that knowledge more effectively.

On the faster front, I have challenged our teams to cut project preparation timelines dramatically. In a world where climate impacts are accelerating and windows of opportunity are narrowing, we cannot afford bureaucratic processes that take years to move from concept to disbursement. We are streamlining our environmental and social safeguards procedures — not by lowering standards, but by eliminating unnecessary steps and leveraging country systems where they meet our requirements.

The IDA replenishment, which will be negotiated over the coming year, is a critical test of the international community's commitment to the poorest countries. IDA provides grants and concessional loans to seventy-five of the world's most vulnerable nations. The current cycle has already been stretched to meet unprecedented demand, and the next replenishment must be significantly larger to match the scale of need. I call on all donors to step up with ambitious pledges.

The debt crisis in developing countries demands urgent attention. More than sixty percent of low-income countries are either in debt distress or at high risk of it. Debt service payments are crowding out investments in health, education, and infrastructure — the very investments that drive long-term growth and poverty reduction. The Common Framework for debt treatment must be made more efficient and predictable, and all creditors must participate in good faith.

Let me say something about fragile and conflict-affected states, which are home to a growing share of the world's extreme poor. By 2030, two-thirds of the world's poorest people are projected to live in fragile settings. The World Bank cannot shy away from these environments. We must find ways to deliver in difficult places, working with humanitarian partners, local organizations, and communities to build resilience and create pathways out of fragility.

Technology and innovation offer tremendous opportunities to leapfrog traditional development pathways. Digital financial services are bringing millions of people into the formal economy. Satellite imagery and artificial intelligence are revolutionizing agricultural monitoring. Clean energy technologies are becoming cost-competitive with fossil fuels in many markets. The World Bank must be a catalyst for the adoption and scaling of these technologies in developing countries.

I want to close with a message about urgency and hope. The challenges we face — poverty, climate change, conflict, inequality — are immense. But they are not insurmountable. We have the knowledge, the resources, and the technology to build a world that is more prosperous, more equitable, and more sustainable. What we need is the collective will to act at scale and at speed.

The World Bank Group is ready to play its part. We are evolving — not for the sake of change, but because the world demands it. I ask our shareholders, our partners, and the development community to join us in this effort. The time for incremental steps has passed. We need transformative action, and we need it now. Thank you.
